Weston walked 450 miles in six days once more in Edinburgh, Scotland, from June 19 to June 25, 1876. He then accomplished what all Great Britain had been waiting for. From September 25 to 30 at the Toxteth Park, in Liverpool, he covered 501 miles within 144 hours.

In the eight months that Weston had been in England he had won $30,000 and made a host of rich, influential friends. One of these was Parliament member Sir John Dugdale Astley, who was known as the "Sporting Baron." Astley was the epitome of the Victorian sporting gentleman. He had a large stable of horses, which he raced often, and he financially supported any athletic endeavor he considered worthy. Weston's performance had so impressed Astley that he offered to back Weston for 500 pounds sterling a side against "any man breathing" in a six-day contest.

Besides stirring the hearts of thousands of spectators, Weston's achievements had stimulated the British pedestrians to realize that there was a great deal of money to be made if an Englishman could defeat Weston. They watched and studied him and gradually learned the intricacies of competing for protracted periods of time. How well they learned their lesson was exhibited by Peter Crossland in Manchester, on September 11 and 12, as he covered 120 miles and 1,500 yards in 24 hours, a world-record performance that caused quite a sensation.

Since Weston had left the United States, O'Leary had dominated the pedestrian scene. His popularity spread from Chicago throughout the United States. In April 1876 he covered 500 miles in 139.5 hours at the Mechanic's Pavilion in San Francisco.

In August, at the American Institute Building in New York City, O'Leary walked 500 miles within six days for the third time. In order to show their appreciation of O'Leary's accomplishments, his friends entertained him at a

banquet at the Union Square Hotel on August 28 and presented him with a gold watch.

The reports of the furor created by Weston in England and the success O'Leary had met with in the United States induced the Irishman to look across the Atlantic. A more personal motive also drove O'Leary 4,000 miles to compete. Weston had told the London press that his six-day match with O'Leary in November 1875 had been tainted by "foul play." Weston claimed that the people in Chicago had thrown pepper in his face and threatened to shoot him. O'Leary was determined to show that he had won fairly and to remove all doubt about who was the best six-day walker in the world.

Upon O'Leary's arrival in England in October 1876 an exhibition was immediately arranged to provide him an opportunity to better Weston's performance of the previous month in Liverpool. The British were still reeling from Weston's walk when O'Leary became the second American within a month to demonstrate the superiority of American pedestrianism, as he walked 502 miles in 143 hours. O'Leary now challenged Weston. Thinking it best to avoid a direct confrontation at this time, Weston refused.

O'Leary had to be content with racing the best British pedestrians. Having observed Weston's technique and training, they were now a far more experienced and toughened set of athletes than Weston had met nine months before. While O'Leary won his first match with Peter Crossland at 300 miles in November of 1876, he was defeated at the same distance by William Howes in December and then again by Crossland in February of 1877.

The Rematch Between Weston and O'Leary

Negotiations for a Weston-O'Leary rematch had been ongoing for several weeks when the articles of agreement were finally signed on January 3, 1877. The two men would walk for six days for 500 pounds each with the winner also receiving two thirds of the gate money and the loser the balance. The editor of Sporting Life was entrusted with all the collected gate money and prize money.

Weston was backed by Sir John Astley, and O'Leary by Sam Hague, an American minstrel manager. The showdown was scheduled for April 2 through April 7, 1877, at Agricultural Hall. The two parties would appoint five judges, and if they could not agree on the rules and their application, the editor of Sporting Life would make the final decision.

Two bands—which were hired to play from 5 A.M. to 12 midnight—would be under the direction of both men at alternate hours and be discontinued while either man was sleeping. The prospect of these two rival champions meeting once again created much excitement on both sides of the Atlantic.

Weston was convinced that anyone completing 506 miles would win, and he was also confident that he would be that person. He wrote out an elaborate time schedule, detailing how far he would travel each day and the exact amount and time of each rest period.

Weston led the first day with 116 miles and 735 yards to O'Leary's 113 miles and 135 yards. The excitement got the better of O'Leary, and he could not sleep the first night. He took only an 84-minute rest. Weston took twice as long and surrendered the lead.

When O'Leary began to build a sizeable lead early on the second day, Astley urged Weston to keep up with the Irishman. Weston refused, and, as the self-assured champion he was, told Astley he was certain that O'Leary would go to pieces and come back to him. But O'Leary was the "gamest of the game," according to Astley, and he maintained his lead throughout the week.

[[FIGURE:1]]

On the fifth day of competition, Weston was getting very leg weary and attempted to relieve the stiffness by "all sorts of styles in his walk." When the band began at 5 A.M., he started off in a "most suspicious manner, which finally resulted in Mr. Watson, the New York judge, objecting to the tallying of a lap." A second judge, Mr. Conquest of Sporting Life, declared the walk fair. This left the ultimate decision up to Captain Webb, the popular long-distance swimmer who had recently crossed the English Channel in 21 hours. He said this about Weston's walking style: "It [isn't] much worse than [what] he has been doing since he started." After Webb delivered this "Solomon-like" opinion, the protest was withdrawn and the lap tallied as Weston continued with his very peculiar gait.

Though O'Leary was completely used up on the morning of the last day, he still led Weston by 10 miles and was increasing his lead as Weston slept in his tent. Astley was sure that all Weston had to do was get back on the track, and the race would be his. He implored Weston to leave his bed. But, as Astley recounts in his autobiography, "He [Weston] went soft, and on my telling him I should chuck some cold water on him, he burst out crying, and that settled the matter; for you can do nothing at any game with a party who pipes his eyes."

O'Leary struggled on, and at 2:05 P.M. he finished his 500th mile. The Chicago Tribune described the scene:

Ladies waved their handkerchiefs and gentlemen threw their hats in the air, while the cheering was perfectly deafening. Bouquets began to arrive and soon the front of O'Leary's tent looked more like a florist's shop than anything else. The ladies who presented these would walk quietly past the barriers and hand them to O'Leary, each gift being the occasion of more vociferous cheering.

At 8:03 P.M. on Saturday, Astley conceded the defeat of Weston and told O'Leary's backers they could take him from the track. But O'Leary continued until he had covered 519 miles and 1,585 yards a minute before 9 P.M. Weston finally completed 510 miles shortly before 10 P.M.

[[FIGURE:2]]

Weston had accomplished what he set out to do—he had bettered the existing world record without totally exhausting himself. He had essentially ignored O'Leary during the competition and had walked his own race. Weston was not disturbed at being defeated, for he knew there would be other days and other races. He wanted to be around to participate in them.

Throughout the week, 70,000 people paid to witness the contest, and vast sums of money were won and lost on the race. Astley himself was covering all bets against Weston 50 pounds to 30 and lost a reported 20,000 pounds or $100,000. But true sportsman that he was, Astley gave Weston a large purse of money for exceeding by four miles the distance he had agreed to do.

The Astley Belt Races Are Born

Astley was so taken with this long-distance business (he said that he was never more excited over any performance) that he decided to inaugurate a series of six-day races for the "Long-Distance Challenge Championship of the World." These races would not be merely walking events but "go-as-you-please" competitions, that is, the pedestrians could walk and/or run in any fashion they chose. Two conflicting reasons are given for the advent of the go-as-you-please concept.

Astley says that he proposed the concept because the peculiar "wobbling gait" of Weston was open to objection as not being a fair heel-and-toe walk. O'Leary gave a different reason. He claimed that since the American pedestrians were so much superior to the English walkers that "it was deemed necessary to invent a style of progression which would place the legitimate champions at a decided disadvantage."

The conditions of the races, which became known as the Astley Belt Races, were originally laid out as follows:

  • The winner would receive a championship belt valued at 100 pounds and a purse of 500 pounds, second place would be worth 100 pounds, and third place, 50 pounds. Other prizes would be awarded to those who succeeded in finishing 450 miles.
  • A certain percentage of the gate money would be divided to defend the belt against any legitimate challenger (as decided by Astley) within three months of the challenge, but the champion would not have to compete more than once very six months.
  • If the defending champion won the belt three times in a row, it would become the permanent possession of that athlete.

The championship belt was made of solid silver and gold. Five plates of silver, plain in the center, with rustic border close around the edges, made up the greater portion of the belt. One gold plate in the center, with the words "Long-Distance Champion of the World" in raised letters, was flanked by a silver plate on each side, similar in shape and border to the others, but bearing, in the center, figures of walking and running, in high relief. The plates were each about two and a half inches in width by three inches in length.

All was now prepared for what was to be one of the most incredible series of athletic contests ever to take place in the arena of sport. The Astley Belt competitions would become the "Gold Age" of pedestrianism.

Chapter 4: The First and Second Astley Belt Races

Monday, March 18, 1878—Agricultural Hall, Islington, London, England. The clock struck 1 A.M. The scorers and judges were in their places. Sir John Astley

stood before 18 of the world's best pedestrians. Some were nervous and fidgety, making last-minute adjustments to their trunks, hats, or shoes. Some stood quietly and serene, almost bored. And a few stood motionless with a look of terror upon their faces, pondering, no doubt, the suffering and pain that was inevitably to come to them by the end of the week.

The vast (500-foot by 300-foot) steel and glass building, lighted only by three chandeliers, looked anything but festive. Two tracks—a seven-lap-to-the-mile one for Englishmen and an eight-lap-to-the-mile one for foreigners—marked the oval center of the building.

[[FIGURE:1]]

The scorers and clerks were alert and ready near the judges' stand. The press, trainers, handlers, and the friends of Sir John (there by special invitation) filled the enclosure in the middle, around which the track was laid. No other observers were present. A New York Times reporter described the surroundings:

The great wide space of benches, set apart for spectators, looked chill and dirty. The light of a charcoal fire in a brazier fell mysteriously upon the small encampment of the pedestrians near the entrance doors. One tent stood alone near the foreigners' track. Among the group of onlookers, the soft hats of Transatlantic visitors were conspicuous.

Sir John Astley addressed the pedestrians this way:

You are about to enter on a trying march, in which running and walking and physical pluck and endurance are necessary to compete. Every possible arrangement has been made to have a fair, straight fight, and I hope the best man will win. I appeal to you to second our efforts, for the best man to win, no matter what his nationality or where he comes from. . . . Now lads are you ready? A fair, honest, manly race, and the best man wins. Ready? Then away you go!

And so the first contest for the Astley Belt began. It had been 11 months since Astley had proposed the six-day, go-as-you-please world championship competition. The English pedestrians had used the time well. Several well-trained men were now capable of defeating the single foreign entry, Daniel O'Leary of the United States. Two in particular had conditioned themselves not only to walk but also to run. They planned to take full advantage of the go-as-you-please provision.

William "Corkey" Gentleman of London, 46 years old and a 25-year veteran of the pedestrian track, was small in stature (5'4", 114 pounds) and had a stooping, awkward gait. He trotted "ungracefully and with a sad look on his face." Henry "Blower" Brown, in contrast, was 6'6", the tallest man in the field, but weighted only 138 pounds. Brown had distinguished himself in his job by the swift manner by which he carried his barrow of bricks to the kiln and back.

Before the race, both Gentleman and Brown were favorites for second place with O'Leary. The bookmakers' choice for first place went to Chester's Henry Vaughan, a carpenter by trade and called by Astley "a real clean-made and thoroughly respectable man." Vaughan was admired by all, even by his fellow competitors, for his "long-striding giant-like" step and the steady pacing of his races.

Vaughan had been one of the first Englishmen to turn in a notable performance as a result of Weston's six-day performance in 1876. In the latter part of that year Vaughan had turned in 100 miles in 18:51:35, and 120 miles in 23:45:00. Both were world records at the time.

Another hometown favorite was the novice William Lewis, 21 years old, of Islington. Just three weeks before the start of the Astley Belt, he was second to William Howes in a 26-hour contest in the same building. In that race Lewis walked 100 miles in 18:37:35 and covered 125 miles and 990 yards in 24 hours. Both of these performances were the best on record except for Howes's performances, in the same races, of 18:08:35 and 127 miles and 1,395 yards, respectively. Lewis, however, was young, inexperienced, and untried at six days.

Weston Sits Out the First Astley

Conspicuously absent from the first Astley Belt race was Edward Payson Weston. After his defeat by O'Leary in April of the previous year, he remained in England and was quite active. In June of 1877 he walked 400 miles in five consecutive days in the Skating Rink at Bradford, and then repeated the performance in July at Bristol, in August at Newcastle-on-Tyne, and in September at Hull.

From December 26, 1877, to January 11, 1878, he walked 1,000 miles in 400 consecutive hours. Weston was in excellent condition and was expected by all to be a challenger for the Astley Belt. Weston had submitted his entry for the first Astley Belt race within the specified time period. O'Leary's entry, however, had been late, and there was some question as to whether he would be permitted to compete. As soon as Astley decided in O'Leary's favor, Weston withdrew, presenting a medical certificate that was regarded by many as "too thin." O'Leary had defeated Weston both times they had met, and the prevailing opinion was that "O'Leary had made him ill."

It was indeed a strange sight as the 18 men set about their awesome task. Some walked, some trotted, some ran. Brown and Gentleman went immediately to the front, with Vaughan and O'Leary following. An incident during the first three hours illustrates some of the brash tactics used during these events.

George Hazael of London began staying right up with O'Leary, "haunting him on the English track like his shadow." Hazael was not, at this time, a well-known pedestrian. His style was described as "hangdog and crouching" in contrast to the "straight, upright, graceful" form of O'Leary.

The athletes were permitted to walk in either the clockwise or counterclockwise direction around the track as long as they switched at the conclusion of a lap. Hazael was walking in the opposite direction of O'Leary. When Hazael passed the Irishman on each curve, he would verbally challenge O'Leary, attempting to get him upset. On one turn Hazael said, "I'll kill this wonderful man before I've done with him."

[[FIGURE:2]]

At the time Hazael was gradually pulling away from O'Leary. Hazael's constant harassment and increasing lead combined to do just as Hazael had hoped. O'Leary became worried and agitated and began to run, much to the dismay of his handlers. During a short rest period his handlers begged O'Leary to stop running. O'Leary responded, "Leave me alone. I know what I am about," and set off again at a run to cut down Hazael's lead.

Hazael had been threatened with disqualification several times for his conduct. The possibility of being disqualified—and the fact that he soon needed all his breath to continue moving—all but silenced the insolent pedestrian. O'Leary, walking and running, relentlessly ran Hazael down, and in the third hour passed him. Hazael quickly fell to the rear and eventually dropped out of the race. O'Leary, a bit nauseous and dizzy from his running, which was unfamiliar to him, finally agreed with his handlers to stop running and resume his standard walking form.

By 5 p.m. on Monday, Gentleman held onto a slight lead of 87 miles to O'Leary's 83. Brown was third with 78 miles, and Vaughan fourth with 77.

The attendance slowly grew during the day so that by 6 p.m. there were 5,000 spectators encouraging the pedestrians on to glory. At 11 p.m. each night the building was closed to all people except the press and a few favored friends. This was usually the time when the pedestrians took the only real rest they allowed themselves. Generally, it amounted to two to four hours in their individual tents that were erected inside the two concentric oval tracks. On the first night, Gentleman, Vaughan, and Brown all retired around 11 p.m. But O'Leary walked on until 2:44 a.m., at which time he had taken over the lead with 124 miles.

Through the second day Gentleman's rapid walking and occasional trotting had overtaken O'Leary, and once more placed "the thin little figure" in front. But by mid-afternoon the running began to tell on Gentleman, and by 7 p.m. O'Leary drew even at 174 miles. Brown wrestled third from Vaughan and led him by a mere mile, 170 to 169.

Gentleman then went in for a short rest. His ever-present wife, who attended to him day and night, was at the door of the tent to meet him with a steaming bowl of ellbroth, his favorite drink. His legs were in great pain, and his short rest turned into one of several hours. By the time Gentleman came back onto the track, he had lost his lead and second place as well. At 6 a.m. Wednesday the score stood as follows: O'Leary with 211 miles, Brown with 206, Gentleman with 196, and Vaughan with 191. There were still 16 men walking, but from fifth place on back, these competitors were far in arrears.

The bookmakers had moved O'Leary into the favorite roll, laying 5-to-3 odds against him. The odds stood at 5-to-2 against Vaughan, 3-to-1 against Gentleman, and 5-to-1 against Brown. O'Leary's backer, Al Smith, the sportsman from Chicago, had wagered several thousand pounds on the Irishman's victory, while Astley and Captain Wyndham were backing Vaughan against all takers.

Throughout the third day O'Leary extended his lead, and Gentleman slipped farther and farther behind. At 1 a.m. Thursday, after three days, it was O'Leary in first with 288 miles, Vaughan and Brown next with 270 miles, and Gentleman, all but used up, with 256 miles.

Public Interest Increases

Interest in this international pedestrian contest increased day by day throughout London. All classes were present among the spectators, from the "titled dukes and lords to the Cheapside hackmen." The various editions of the newspapers were bought up as fast as they were printed so hourly bulletins could be sent out from Agricultural Hall. Inside the hall the excitement was intense. The cheers often drowned out the music from the band with men dancing and waving their hats.

Vaughan kept within striking distance of O'Leary throughout Thursday and Friday (13 to 16 miles behind). Late Friday afternoon O'Leary finally showed the first signs of weakening. His left leg began to swell, and a slight limp was noticeable. This was Vaughan's chance. But he, too, was suffering. Both feet had been blistered badly since Wednesday, and they were now swathed in cotton wool. The hope in the Vaughan camp was that he could continue all Friday night, especially when O'Leary went in to rest.

As the damp night air seeped into the vast arena, O'Leary occasionally staggered, and his knees trembled. Vaughan pressed on. Spurred by O'Leary's deteriorating condition, Vaughan started to run. For a short time he was able to keep up lap for lap with O'Leary, even though Vaughan was on the seven-lap track and O'Leary on the eight-lap track. At 7 P.M. Vaughan got within 12.5 miles of O'Leary, 426 miles to 438.5.

O'Leary rested from 11:30 P.M. Friday until 3 A.M. Saturday morning. Vaughan, sticking to his plan, rested only at intervals throughout the early morning hours. When O'Leary returned to the track, legs stiff and sore, feet in pain, he still managed to walk at a steady pace. He was cheered on by those present, especially by the Irish members of Parliament and their wives. O'Leary was able to increase his lead on Vaughan, who never again was a challenge.

At 3:30 A.M. on Saturday O'Leary completed his 497th mile; Vaughan was 24 miles behind. The closing hours were depicted by the New York Time's London correspondent this way:

It was a sorry sight the last few hours of the week's walk. Haggard, dazed, staggering O'Leary, his arms no longer knitted in pedestrian form and braced with muscular strength, were limp and almost helpless. His legs were swollen. He went his miserable round in evident pain, though 15,000 cheered him on. Vaughan went on until 7:30 Saturday night, and retired at the solicitation of his friends when he had scored 500 miles. O'Leary at this time was 19 miles ahead, and he was determined to make his record in round numbers, 520 miles, beating, it is said, all his achievements on both sides of the Atlantic.

[[FIGURE:1]]

At the close of the race, the figures were O'Leary with 520.25 miles, Vaughan with 500 miles, and Brown with 477.25 miles. There were no others walking at the end.

O'Leary Earns the Astley Belt

O'Leary came away with the Astley Belt and a total of $3,750 in prize and gate money. He took the belt to the United States and declared he would not part with it until "some better man came and fetched it." Vaughan received $1,300 and Brown $525. It was estimated that a total of $500,000 was wagered during the race.

As it had done after O'Leary's other two triumphs, the Chicago Tribune editorialized the following day:

Chicago is ahead again. Chicago is always ahead. The splendid record that O'Leary has made in his walk in London is something of which every Chicagoan has a right to be proud. It has been a great time for the United States and for Chicago and "a great day for Ireland." Daniel O'Leary went over to England without any blowing of trumpets or waving of flags. He has not made a public exhibition of himself after the style of Weston. He left us quietly to take part in a walking match in which a score or more of the best pedestrians in the United Kingdom were his competitors, trusting to his legs rather than his tongue for success. And he has used them to such good advantage that today the one name that is blown over the world through the trumpet of fame is O'Leary.

. . . How sudden are the changes of fortune! Five little years ago no one knew O'Leary except the servant-maids on his postal route in this city, to whom he brought every morning good or bad tidings from absent swains. Little they dreamed that O'Leary was to be one of the heroes of the world, the first question in the morning and the last at night, the burden of every newsboy's yell, and the theme of discussion in every social gathering. Little they thought of the fame those legs that went plodding along from house to house would achieve. Little he thought, perhaps, that the time would come when his name would go flashing along the oozy bed of the ocean day after day, and that two Continents would hang upon that one word O'Leary. . . .

The remarkable victory of O'Leary will not alone be a triumph of legs, but of brain. It will not alone be the result of physical endurance, but of mental and moral endurance also. It requires courage, patience, will, and intellect—a mighty concentration of willpower upon a certain object to be attained.

If, further, it is thrown out that all these qualities were exerted for money, it may be answered that all efforts are made for money, and that without the spur of money the world would stand still, stagnate, and rot. . . .

We are inclined, therefore, to congratulate our wiry little O'Leary upon the splendid exhibition of Chicago brawn and brain that he has made, and that he has beaten the Briton in the very class of exercise for which the latter has always been famous; and Chicago will congratulate herself that she is ahead again as usual, however much it may add to the discomfort of St. Louis and her other suburbs.

O'Leary sailed home, with a stopover in Ireland, and returned to Chicago May 19, 1878.

O'Leary Is Challenged

When Weston set the world record for the six-day race in 1876, O'Leary was quick to challenge him. So now, several challenges came to O'Leary in rapid succession. Vaughan, Gentleman, and Brown all wanted another chance at O'Leary in London. O'Leary refused, saying that he would walk for the belt only in the United States.

Soon after arriving home, O'Leary was challenged by John Hughes, another Irishman, from New York City. Hughes was prompted not only by a desire to be the champion but also by an intense personal antipathy for O'Leary. When O'Leary first went East in 1875, Hughes wanted to compete with him but could find no one to back him. He continually boasted to all who would listen that if he only had the money he could easily defeat O'Leary at any distance. Hughes's constant harangue greatly annoyed O'Leary. When O'Leary returned to the East in 1878 to sail for the Astley Belt, Hughes told O'Leary he would come to England and beat him but he had no money. O'Leary replied sarcastically, "I'll build you a bridge." This so nettled Hughes that whenever he talked of O'Leary after this he would always say bitterly, "the rapscallion told me he'd build me a bridge!"

As soon as word arrived of O'Leary's winning performance in England, Hughes eagerly sought someone to back an attempt to surpass O'Leary's six-day effort in the Astley Belt. He finally found Harry Hill, who wagered former 100-yard champion Pargh Davis $1,000 that Hughes would be able to better O'Leary's mark for six days. Hughes competed alone in his attempt to walk farther than O'Leary. A trial against time was set up for April 21 to 27, 1878, at the Central Park Garden. The track, almost oval in shape, was partly in the hall of the Garden and partly outside in the Garden. Hughes's confidence in his ability far outstripped his actual ability. He set out at a suicidal pace, covering the first mile in 6:22. He covered the first 5 miles in 36:25, 25 miles in 3:05:10, and 30 miles in 3:41:45. Then, as would be expected, he crashed.

Hughes did finish 100 miles for the day, but he had destroyed all chances of success in the first hours. Somehow he managed to last out the six days and eventually finished 389 miles and 3,485 feet. His handlers said that this was accomplished with no real training and that he would now begin professional training for another attempt at O'Leary's record. This Hughes did, and he was reported to have completed 500 miles in six days in Newark later that year. The result was doubted by the sporting press, however.

Hughes now formally challenged O'Leary under the Astley Belt rules. O'Leary considered Hughes a mere "speculator" and not worthy of a contest. Hughes persisted, and Sir John Astley finally decreed that O'Leary must meet Hughes or forfeit the belt. The match was set for Gilmore's Garden at Madison Square from September 30 to October 5, 1878.

At the start O'Leary struck out on a long, body-swinging walk, while Hughes, having learned little from his failure at Central Park, dashed off on a run, making five miles in 35:41 to O'Leary's 53:14. O'Leary was on the outer one-eighth mile track, Hughes on the inner one-ninth mile track. Members of the Harlem Athletic Club kept the scores in three separate notebooks, each lap being called out distinctly to both men. Hughes was handled by his family, trainer, and backers, and occupied a tent at one end of the Garden. O'Leary used one of the rooms in the main building. It was clear early on in the competition that O'Leary was working simply to beat Hughes and had no concern for records. O'Leary refused to believe that Hughes had fairly covered 500 miles in Newark. The match had little excitement and was concluded at 11 P.M. Saturday. O'Leary won easily with 310 miles.

O'Leary had now won two matches. If he won one more challenge match, he would become the permanent owner of the Astley Belt. The English

pedestrians anxiously inquired among themselves about who should be the man fortunate enough to regain the coveted prize.

Vaughan was considered by many as the obvious choice. Had it not been for his badly blistered feet, he may have beaten O'Leary in the first Belt race in London. Others thought either Gentleman or Brown would be better selections since they were still convinced it would take a runner to beat O'Leary.

This opinion was strengthened when Astley sponsored a go-as-you-please race for the "Astley Belt and Long-Distance Champion of England" at the end of October 1878.

This race was won by the "runner," William "Corkey" Gentleman, who equaled O'Leary's distance of 520 miles and 440 yards in 138 hours—one hour fewer than O'Leary. Harry "Blower" Brown, another runner, was second with 505 miles and 754 yards. This was only the third time that two men had both surpassed 500 miles in the same race.

The problem of regaining the "World" Astley Belt title intensified for the Brits when it was learned that two Americans, John Ennis of Chicago and Charles Harriman of Massachusetts, had already challenged O'Leary. Preliminary arrangements were, in fact, already being made. A competent English challenger had to be found.

In addition to the two Astley Belt races, O'Leary had walked in seven other races or exhibitions in 1878 for a total of 3,110 miles. He was "raced out." His advisors suggested a long rest. O'Leary, seeing the wisdom of their advice, left the North and went to the hot springs of Arkansas. His rest was cut short, however, by Sir John Astley's call that he defend the Championship Belt.

Reprinted with permission of Ed Dodd.

The illustrations on pages 92, 94, 96, and 99 were created for this revised edition of The Great Six-Day Races.

Part III of The Great Six-Day Races will continue in the next issue with

Chapter 5: The Third Astley Belt Race

Chapter 6: The Fourth Astley Belt Race

articles.title: San Francisco Marathon
articles.body_html:
SAN FRANCISCO is the most distinctive city in the United States. It is a conglomeration of diverse neighborhoods and panoramic vistas famous throughout the world. Annually it is chosen by leading travel magazines as the favorite destination within U.S. borders. To call San Francisco "colorful" would be an understatement.

San Francisco is ever changing and evolving, a city that attempts to put its best foot forward and cover the scuff marks on the shoe. For more than two decades, the San Francisco Marathon has been changing and evolving as well, while showing both its shine and its bruises.

Certainly, the marathon has a lot going for it. Where else can you find a midsummer marathon with cool weather? And in what other city in the United States can you find a course that passes by such spectacular scenery and some of the nation's richest ethnic and architectural neighborhoods?

Unfortunately, at the same time, the indifference and occasional hostility of what newspaper columnist Herb Caen calls the "cool, gray city" is reflected in the meager public support for the race. Among urban marathons, San Francisco has probably the smallest crowds of spectators imaginable.

Just as San Francisco can be considered the largest small town in the United States, its marathon can be considered the country's largest small marathon. The New York City and Los Angeles marathons measure their entrants in the tens of thousands. San Francisco has been averaging 3,000 participants.

A GEM TO BE DISCOVERED

Because of its beautiful (but challenging) course, ideal midsummer running weather, and setting amid one of the most attractive regions of the country, the San Francisco Marathon is a gem waiting to be discovered by the masses of runners who flock to other big-city marathons.

The megamarathons in other U.S. cities command huge sponsorships and competitive fields. San Francisco has never attracted a long-term major sponsor, and these days it attracts no big-name competitors. For better or worse—and we think better—San Francisco enables amateurs to compete and stand out among themselves.

San Francisco has the potential to be a major player in the marathon sweepstakes, but a number of diverse factors would have to come together, and that may never happen. The race made an attempt at megastardom in the wake of the 1984 Olympics when it drew 10,000 runners and produced Simeon Kigen's record of 2:10:17. But we don't foresee those kinds of numbers in the near future, even though the July 13, 1997, running will be the race's 20th anniversary.

COURSE HISTORY

To understand the current San Francisco Marathon course, you need to know a bit about the race's history. Originated in 1977 by the city's family-oriented Pamakid Runners (Pa-Ma-Kids. Get it?), the race originally confined itself to loops in beautiful Golden Gate Park, along the ocean beaches, and around Lake Merced at the city's remote southwestern corner. The race offered no prize money and generally helped create local heroes and heroines—Olympian Nancy Ditz, for example.

The course setting was entirely scenic, even pastoral, but it offered none of the diversity of San Francisco's exciting neighborhoods. In fact, the course avoided neighborhoods entirely. This changed in 1982 when invigorated leadership within the Pamakids combined with a major sponsorship to produce a city-wide course that showed off Chinatown, North Beach, the waterfront, and Golden Gate Park. For three years, the race doubled in size annually, reaching its apex one week after the Los Angeles Olympic Games.

The marathon's success, however, proved to be its undoing in the complex mileau of San Francisco politics. While the runners appreciated their journey through ethnic and scenic venues, the shopkeepers and residents in those neighborhoods found the race a nuisance, in spite of the fact that many of the runners returned to the same neighborhoods after the race was finished to spend money on meals and tourist attractions.

In a city where all voices are heard and accommodated, the complaints of a handful were ultimately sufficient to produce political opposition among several of the elected city supervisors, which did nothing to maintain a major sponsor.

At the same time, in an effort to respond to a chorus of opponents, the marathon was forced to change its course for the 1985 edition (hence, the lack of any real meaning to a San Francisco Marathon course record). The race bottomed out in 1988 when the lack of sponsorship caused a one-year cancellation. Fortunately, in the years that followed, folks with more political savvy assumed control of the race, and with more aggressive marketing, were able to bring it back to a semblance of life, if not glory. Beginning in 1989, the Pamakids' expertise combined with the resources of these new leaders to design the existing course that remains as spectacular as ever, but more importantly, is politically acceptable.

THE "NEW" COURSE

The San Francisco Marathon course is designed for two purposes: to give the runners as much scenic and urban variety as possible and to get the runners in and out of the most interesting and spectacular urban areas as early as possible to avoid rubbing store owners the wrong way. To accomplish this task, the race begins at 8 A.M. on the Marin County (north) side of the Golden Gate Bridge, then passes through the Presidio, Fisherman's Wharf, North Beach (the Italian neighborhood), and Chinatown well before 9 A.M. For the runners, the experience is like waltzing through a city in the wake of a neutron bombing. Between miles 5 and 10, they travel through some of the nation's most unique and attractive neighborhoods, but the streets are totally devoid of human beings!

Between miles 8 and 12 the race detours far south of Market Street to avoid interfering with public surface transportation. Some runners dislike this diversion into the city's industrial zone, but we approve of these few miles in which race participants can encounter and experience the hardy but unglamorous nontourist side of San Francisco.

At mile 12, while passing through the Mission District, and at mile 13, when the race encounters its major hill of 200 feet, which ascends into the famed hippie Haight-Ashbury district, runners once again find themselves in interesting urban communities—by then peopled with locals and tourists taking turns sipping coffee and watching marathoners.

At 14 miles, the half-mile of Haight Street between Masonic Boulevard and Golden Gate Park is the only segment of the race where crowds and street scenes even begin to evoke the urban courses of New York and

The Basics

San Francisco Marathon

FOR INFO ON SAN FRANCISCO: San Francisco Visitor Info Center, 900 Market near Powell, 415/391-2000. Web address: http://www.citysearch7.com/

GETTING THERE: San Francisco is one of the most visited cities in the United States and is served by dozens of domestic and international airlines. Although driving to San Francisco is easy from the north (take Highway 101), the east (take I-80), or the south (take Highway 101 and then I-280), once you've arrived, the city is not automobile friendly. Parking is limited, and parking garages are expensive. Fortunately, no other city in America has the variety of in-city public transportation that San Francisco has—everything from electric buses and BART to cable cars that "go halfway to the stars." San Francisco, 700,000 in population, is very walker friendly, and many of the city's famous landmarks are within an easy stroll of the official race hotel.

[[FIGURE:1]]

Boston. Just beyond the famous Haight-Ashbury intersection at mile 14.7, San Francisco running institution Hoys Sports offers Gu to the runners, a custom that deserves repetition elsewhere.

Once across Stanyan Street at mile 15.5, the remainder of the race is run in Golden Gate Park and along scenic Sunset Boulevard. No crowds or cheers encourage racers until they reach the finish at the "new" Kezar Stadium, situated a mere long block from where the race entered the park some 11 miles earlier.

THE FINISH

Many runners appreciate the broad avenues that belong entirely to them as they wind through the magnificent park. And all runners give high marks for the fact that at the race's conclusion at the city's western edge, the course guarantees maximum exposure to fog and comforting marine air at the warmest time of the day in the Bay Area.

The Kezar Stadium venue enables the marathon to finish Olympic-style with runners taking a three-quarter lap around the state-of-the-art track. This splendid and newly reconstructed stadium accommodates waiting friends and families. There is a price to pay for the luxury, however. Kezar Stadium is located at the eastern, highest end of Golden Gate Park. To reach it, runners must make a gradual 100-foot ascent over the race's final three miles.

[[FIGURE:2]]

The course shows off much, if not all, of San Francisco's distinctive scenery and marvelous diversity. Hills are unavoidable in a city built on at least seven of them, but the race confines and minimizes their impact as effectively as is possible, given the need to finish the event in Golden Gate Park and away from the downtown.

About the only thing lacking in San Francisco that is apparent at other urban marathons are crowds of spectators. But that is a condition that will likely remain. Most San Franciscans prefer to participate in their own activity than spectate someone else's.

THE START

The San Francisco Marathon is privileged. It remains the only footrace in San Francisco for which Golden Gate Bridge traffic is diverted so runners can begin their race on the mile-long magic carpet leading into the city. But the logistics of such a spectacular start exact a price. You need to board buses between 6:00 and 6:30 A.M. to get to that start.

Like the New York City Marathon, San Francisco cannot allow private vehicles to transport runners to the staging area. But unlike New York, runners are not forced to wait at the staging area for hours as a result. Runners wait at the vista point on the Marin County side of the bridge for about an hour—enough time to find your friends and use the port-a-johns.

Bus service to the start has proven extremely reliable in recent San Francisco Marathons. You can either leave from the headquarters hotel (the Marriott) or from the finish area at Kezar Stadium at the southeast corner of Golden Gate Park. There is no need for out of towners to bring a car. However, if you or family members do drive to the Stadium, heed this warning: There is generally ample parking around the stadium, but do not park in a prohibited area (you will get towed, even on a Sunday), and do not park with your wheels on park grass (you will receive an expensive ticket, especially on a Sunday). Your best bet? For five dollars, use the pay lot off Stanyan Street between Waller and Lincoln.

[[FIGURE:3]]

Dress for the Weather

Because of the hour-long wait at the staging area, warm clothing is a must. Recall Mark Twain's observation of weather in San Francisco—"The coldest winter I ever experienced was a summer in San Francisco." If it's a typical foggy morning, about the coldest spot in the Bay Area is at the Golden Gate Bridge. But you can shed the warm-ups for the race, put them in a numbered race bag, and meet them at the finish line. Even if fog prevails for the entire race, a singlet may still be the right shirt choice for this benign, air-conditioned climate. Unlike the warm-up clothes retrieval fiasco at Boston Common in 1996, it'll take you a matter of seconds to retrieve your warm sweats at the Kezar finish area.

THE PRELIMINARIES

In recent years, the San Francisco Marriott has served as race headquarters. Its bizarre semi-neoclassical architecture has yet to be accepted by natives of the City by the Bay. The hotel is less than kindly referred to locally as the Marriott Wurlitzer, but its prime location at Market and Fourth Streets serves the out of towner well. The hotel is located at stops for both BART and MUNI (the regional and municipal transit systems), which enable visitors to forego renting a car while in town.

Close by the hotel is Union Square, with our country's most compact and eclectic collection of shopping institutions surrounding it on all sides. Also at the hotel's doorstep is the main terminal of the cable car line, which can carry runners to Nob Hill, Russian Hill, and Aquatic Park, all places deserving of a visit on foot and all offering a variety of dining opportunities.

Moreover, for richer dining and entertainment, the fit marathon runner can walk north to Chinatown and North Beach, or south and east to the new Yerba Buena Center and the city's newest "signature" building, the Museum of Modern Art. The more adventurous visitor will want to explore beyond Yerba Buena to the energetic South of Market (SOMA) and South Beach neighborhoods, which contain the city's most forward-looking night spots and residential communities.

For a race that is at best midsized, the San Francisco Marathon presents a respectable expo at the Marriott. The race also features a carbo load at the Marriott on the eve of the marathon, but only the most unimaginative visitor could fail to find far more interesting cuisine in Chinatown, North Beach, or better yet, in the less touristy neighborhoods of the Fillmore District, Cole Valley, Irving Street, or Noe Valley—all of which can be reached easily by MUNI from the race headquarters hotel.

THE GOODIES

When the San Francisco Marathon T-shirt and poster are good, they are among the best in the sport. The 1996 T-shirt immediately became a classic for its elegant Golden Gate Bridge design and subtle appearance of only one sponsor's logo. The accompanying 1996 poster was the best bargain we've ever seen in quality running art. An individual silk-screen copy costs $22 and is already showing up in San Francisco galleries! (The 1997 poster looks equally impressive.) The poster is also replicated in a postcard—by far the most attractive marathon postcard we've ever seen. If future years stick to this standard, runners will be proud to show off their San Francisco Marathon mementos.

The prerace program and results booklet, however, are another matter. They are as weak as the T-shirt and poster are strong. The program has a few statistics slipped in between the sponsors' ads. Your local throw-away newspaper sets the quality standard for the results booklet—actually it's just part of the USATF regional bimonthly newsletter.

Perhaps with the 20th anniversary this year, race management will surprise us with improved publications.

EVERY major city in the world features an array of attractions that make it unique and that enlighten and entertain visitors. And, because of its very size, every major city is saddled with areas it would rather have vanish in a puff of smoke. The following is a brief consideration of both.

GO SEE IT

Golden Gate Park. Along with Central Park in New York City and Stanley Park in Vancouver, at over 1,000 acres, this is one of the most beautiful urban parks in North America. It's a they-said-it-couldn't-be-done park laid down on top of sandhills that everyone predicted would never take to sod. The park is a runner's paradise, with rolling hills, ever-changing vistas, paved roads, asphalt walkways, and wooded trails. The park is three miles long and features everything from a fly-casting pond and a model boat pool to a rose garden and a buffalo herd. Within the park there are also some excellent must-see attractions: the Conservatory of Flowers, the M.H. de Young Memorial Museum, the Asian Art Museum, the California Academy of Sciences, the Steinhart Aquarium and Morrison Planetarium, the Japanese Tea Garden, and the Strybing Arboretum and Botanical Gardens. Since the race ends in Golden Gate Park, a walk around and through its attractions is a great way to walk the stiffness out of your legs after the race.

North Beach. If you like good, strong coffee, superb pasta, and a delightful neighborhood, the Italian-American North Beach is the place to visit. The center of North Beach is Washington Square—bordered by a cathedral, the Italian-American Athletic Club, Fiore d'Italia, and the Washington Square Bar & Grill—a peaceful patch of green within the heart of the city where you can relax on a park bench and lick ice cream while watching old men play bocce. It's hard to find a bad cup of coffee or a bad restaurant in the North Beach area. This is the same place where Mark Twain hung out in the 1860s and where Jack Kerouac and the Beats did their thing in the 1950s. It is also probably one of the safest after-dark neighborhoods of any metropolis in the United States.

Museum of Modern Art. When the museum opened in early 1995, its architecture joined the Transamerica Pyramid and the Marriott as the subjects of controversy. Some love the Modernist lines and the atrium inside the building, while others consider the place an ugly monolith. It is located at 151 Third near Mission (only two blocks from the official race hotel), and admission is $7. Art and photographic exhibits come and go, but there are several permanent collections, such as "From Matisse to Diebenkorn: Works From the Permanent Collection of Painting and Sculpture."

Noe Valley. Originally known as Nanny Goat Hill, later named (in 1846) after the last Mexican alcalde, this is a lovely, pleasant, Victorian-enriched neighborhood that's out of the way of tourist buses and offers a well-sheltered escape from the hectic life downtown—even though it isn't far from downtown. You'll find a surplus of good restaurants and lots of pleasant strolling territory.

City Lights Books. Located at 261 Columbus, which is the intersection of Chinatown, North Beach, and the sleazy porn houses, the bookstore was founded by Lawrence Ferlinghetti in 1953 and served as the magnet of the Beat Generation. The store features one of the best inventories of Beat literature in existence. City Lights also publishes its own line of poetry books. It's a wonderful place to browse, where you may come across that unexpected treat—a book you didn't even know existed but that you've just gotta have.

South of Market (especially southern Embarcadero). A decade ago we'd have felt compelled to warn folks about the South of Market area, but redevelopment has been turning this section of the city into a gem. We urge you to soak up this section of town, which is a radical departure from the rest of the city. Walk from Yerba Buena through South Beach, down to the new developments and boulevard along the refurbished southern Embarcadero.

AVOID IT

Tenderloin. This area of town (north of Market, between Taylor and Larkin streets) is pretty seedy, especially after dark. This is a good place to avoid, especially since there is so much worth seeing elsewhere.

Fisherman's Wharf shops. The ultimate tourist trap, Fisherman's Wharf has stores lining the streets screaming at you to buy stuff that you'll chuck into a closet and forget when you get home—or throw into the next clothing drive or garage sale. Again, there is so much to see and do in San Francisco—you don't need to waste your time here.

Union Square. Union Square is a square block of grass with an underground parking garage down where the roots live. It used to be surrounded by elegant department stores, but Union Square has been undergoing a sort of "de-vival" with the arrival of places like Virgin Records, Nike Town, and other contemporary merchants. These are mere shadows of the grand stores that used to grace this little enclave.

But don't come to San Francisco expecting prerace or postrace materials that equal those of the Boston or New York City marathons.

A BARGAIN, BUT . . .

If a runner signs up early (before Memorial Day), San Francisco remains a bargain at $35. Like Boston and New York, San Francisco also requires a $15 USATF membership, which in San Francisco's case can seem a bit like extortion, since the USATF actually presents the race. However, we believe that membership in our sport's governing body is a good idea, especially now that the USATF may be paying more attention to the needs of long distance running. But even if you don't philosophically support the USATF, the marathon is well worth the $50 total.

When the San Francisco Marathon went city wide in 1982, its founders and many outside observers (including Fred Lebow) expected it to become part of a Big Three with New York and Boston. That expectation has never been fulfilled, in part because of local politics, lack of consistent major sponsorship, and because big-name runners have other summer commitments (i.e., the Olympics and world championships).

But given its location in "everybody's favorite city," its ideal climate for a midsummer race, and its timing halfway between Patriots' Day and Halloween, the San Francisco Marathon does remain the logical third race for urban marathoners.

Now that the race has performed well for several years as a medium-sized event, it should not be long before sponsors and runners from around the nation and the globe raise it to the stature of a major event.

Runner's Highs/Runner's Lows

HIGHS LOWS
Delightful, exciting city Hills on the course
Outstanding T-shirts and poster Atrocious race program with little useful information but lots of ads
Extremely scenic course Spectators few and far between
Compact expo
Golden Gate Bridge start
Kezar Stadium "Olympic"-like finish

The Bottom Line

We have weighed various aspects of a marathon within a 1,000-point scoring grid. Besides the author of the article, a dozen runners at the race were randomly chosen to score the race for us. (SFM = San Francisco Marathon.) The results follow:

1. HISTORY/TRADITION

Evaluate the race's sense of history and tradition.
[Possible points: 30 SFM score: 20]

2. ENTRY FORM

Is the race entry form clear, concise, attractive, complete, and easy to fill out?
[Possible points: 20 SFM score: 18]

3. ENTRY COST

For most races, the entry fee covers between 30 and 50 percent of the cost of putting on the event. Rate the value of your dollar relative to this race.
[Possible points: 30 SFM score: 24]

4. LOCALE/SCENICS

Is the race held in an area that is easy to get to and scenic, offers adequate food and housing services, and nonrace activities for family and friends?
[Possible points: 50 SFM score: 49]

5. REGISTRATION

Is registration well-organized and efficient? Does it bog down unnecessarily?
[Possible points: 20 SFM score: 18]

6. PRERACE ACTIVITIES

Evaluate activities such as pasta feeds, parties, and so on, during the days before the race.
[Possible points: 50 SFM score: 36]

7. EXPO

Does the expo offer a fair number and variety of booths relative to the race's size? Are there quality exhibitors and good guest speaker(s).
[Possible points: 50 SFM score: 34]

8. COURSE

Take into consideration the following: degree of difficulty, certified, sanctioned, quality of road or trail surface, adequate mileage and directional

markers, aid stations, medical coverage, race communications, accessibility to course for friends and family, typical weather, and so on.
[Possible points: 400 SFM score: 337]

9. RACE AMENITIES

This category includes race T-shirt, finisher's medal, finisher's certificate, adequate and efficient finish area, ease of sweatbag retrieval, showers, postrace refreshments, awards ceremony, raffles, results postcard, results book, and so on.
[Possible points: 250 SFM score: 188]

10. VOLUNTEERS

Are the volunteers experienced and adequate in number?
[Possible points: 100 SFM score: 80]

TOTAL SCORE FOR SAN FRANCISCO MARATHON:

804 points (out of 1,000)

articles.title: Letters
articles.body_html:
PREMIER ISSUE

It's refreshing to read a publication that deals with running and fitness without trying to sell you a lot of products. I enjoyed Joe Oakes's article, "Walking the Walk," which discussed taking walking breaks during training and racing. Intuitively, many people know walking is important, but our egos get in the way of our listening to what our bodies are trying to tell us. I have run races in which I was exhausted, but I vowed to keep running, "no matter what." The article gave me several new training ideas.

Dr. Johnson's article on analgesics was also great. I'm a nurse, and I thought I knew a lot about anti-inflammatory agents, but this article was full of information I wasn't aware of. I hope people read this article and take it to heart. Running is about health, fitness, and enjoyment. Too many sports involve drug use. Running shouldn't be one of them.

Mario Molinari
Santa Rosa, Calif.

I found Marathon & Beyond to be not only comprehensive reading for the serious distance runner but also a refreshing approach to a subject that has long been sparsely covered. This publication adds a new seriousness to endurance running. The first issue hit home with me, but I look forward to your next issue when you'll deal with the Boston Marathon, one of my favorite subjects!

Jack Anderson
Eugene, Ore.

Congratulations on a very successful and enjoyable first issue! It was refreshing to read a publication that didn't have a mind-numbing article about which "Bay Watch" star jogs on the beach. Here's hoping M&B grows to be a literary force within the running community.

I was greatly impressed by John Keston's recounting of his Valencia Marathon. His accuracy in describing the inner turmoil that precedes a race struck a familiar cord. I could easily relate to the conflicting feelings of hunger to race and fear of disappointment. Goals that seemed so realistic during training suddenly seem somewhat intimidating. Keston's account captures the intense pleasures of racing the marathon.

Although I found "Analgesics: Cure or Curse?" very informative, I believe that Dr. Johnson only briefly touched on the real issue. We all want something, anything that will suddenly end the pain and get us back training as soon as possible. We live in a quick-fix society where if there's a pill that might fix our problems, then we better swallow two or three of them. The real issue behind overuse injuries is not what pill will cure the ill, but what message our body is trying to tell us. Taking away the inflammation or pain leaves the cause of the injury unresolved. It distracts us from the message that the body is trying to convey: "Don't increase your mileage that fast" or "Run on a softer surface" or "Buy orthotics" or "Strengthen and stretch your weak links." Overuse injuries don't happen without cause. As Dr. Johnson briefly mentions, the cure to an injury is not found in the medicine cabinet, but rather in locating and repairing the cause.

Bruce Decon
Victoria, British Columbia

I very much enjoyed the letter from Pheidippides as well as the rest of the first issue of Marathon & Beyond. I am looking forward to using the run/walk method in the Houston Marathon. I walked briskly in the latter miles of my last marathon, when I started to slow down to running 12-minute miles. I walked for a minute and ran the rest of the mile, and my pace became 10 minutes per mile. This strategy enabled me to record my fastest marathon of the year.

In the front of M&B, the mission statement says that it is not the publication's intention to give locations, dates, and so on of marathons and ultramarathons. This is fine regarding marathons since Runner's World does a fine job of providing this information, but I don't know where to go to find information on ultramarathons. Can you help?

Tom Detore
Wilber, Neb.

Ultramarathoning results, feature articles, and schedules for upcoming races can be found in the 15-year-old and still running Ultrarunning magazine, P.O. Box 2120, Amherst, MA 01004-2120. A year's U.S. subscription is $25.— Ed.

articles.title: On THE Mark
articles.body_html:
1. WEIGHT TRAINING FOR MARATHONERS.

I have read that weight training is beneficial to marathon training. When in the training schedule should weights be introduced? What exercises should I do? What reps and weights?
Chris Cocek, Edmonton, Alberta

STRENGTH TRAINING is beneficial to any physical activity. Strength can be improved with free weights, weight machines, or your own body weight. Use whichever of these methods is most convenient for you. You want a balanced program that improves the strength in both your upper and lower body. A program that uses your own body weight as resistance in doing push-ups, pull-ups, dips, sit-ups, and curls would be beneficial. For weighted exercise, start with low weights and proceed slowly, gradually building the reps and weights for each exercise. Recent research seems to indicate that one "set" of weight work two to four times per week is probably adequate for marathoners.

—Dr. William Roberts
is a member of the Marathon & Beyond Science Advisory Board. He specializes in sports medicine at MinnHealth SportsCare Consultants in White Bear Lake, Minnesota.

YOU CAN strengthen the arms, back, and shoulders by lifting light weights at high repetitions. Universal equipment works well as an apparatus. Doing three sets of 10 repetitions on the Universal machine every other day throughout the year will help strengthen various muscle groups. In addition, you can incorporate free bar lifting, which focuses on the triceps, shoulders, and pectoral muscles. Again, always use low weights with quick reps.

—Mark Conover
is a coach at Cal Poly San Luis Obispo, in California, a running columnist for The Runner's Schedule, and the winner of the 1988 U.S. Olympic Marathon Trials.

2. RUNNING TO 80—AND BEYOND.

I am currently 62 years old and hope to run into my 80s. My question has two parts: What advice can you give me so I can successfully enjoy my next 20 years of running? And do older runners have an increased incidence of arthritis?
Nester Slywka, Burnaby, British Columbia

RUNNING IN your age group (which is also mine) can be enjoyable and very beneficial to health, so it is increasingly important as time goes by for you to "listen to your body" and run within comfortable limits. If you run competitively, run against people in your age group. If you feel unwell or have unusual symptoms, stop running and consult a physician—preferably one sympathetic to older runners!

A number of studies indicate that older runners generally have fewer symptoms of arthritis than do sedentary people of the same age. However, signs and symptoms of arthritis do increase for aging runners and nonrunners. Moderate exercise has an important place in arthritis therapy. However, data on people aged 65 and over who run marathons and beyond are, understandably, quite limited.

—Dr. Peter Wood,
recently retired from Stanford University, is the veteran of dozens of marathons, and is chairman of this publication's Science Advisory Board.

I'M WITH you, Nester! At 72 I'm still enjoying my running and plan to run into my 90s or longer. Here's my recipe. For maximum enjoyment, run with friends. Doing so enlivens the exercise experience and keeps you from becoming bored. The miles pass quicker, and there is lots to talk about. When running on your own, have a workout planned ahead and don't deviate, except for unforeseen circumstances. Make sure you get lots of rest, too, but not necessarily on your duff. Walking days are great rest times and much better than doing nothing. Four or five miles at a 15- to 16-minute pace will repair the stresses of harder running days. You should also race at least a couple of times a month to stay motivated and share the camaraderie of others who care about their wellness. Remind yourself often that you would rather be running and feeling good about yourself than having bypass surgery or having your arteries routed out.

Older runners generally suffer less from arthritis than do sedentary seniors. More medical professionals these days are recommending exercise to minimize the problems of arthritis. Occasional use of anti-inflammatories is okay. Keep moving, perfuse the joints with fresh, clean, fast-flowing blood, and you'll be running well into your 80s.

—John Keston
is the oldest man in the world to run a sub-3:00 marathon.

RUNNING IS an excellent exercise to help preserve both your mental and physical health, and you have every right to hope to continue to derive enjoyment from running as you age. However, you must also recognize that your running and racing pace will gradually slow down as you get older, so mentally you must face the fact that your PRs are probably long past. What you want to do is slow down the rate at which you slow down. Put another way, you want to be "all that you can be" for your age.

Although how fast you slow down depends primarily on your genetics and your training, one factor that may accelerate the rate of decline is injury. Aging appears to slow down one's recovery from running injuries. Thus, one primary way to continue to enjoy your running is to prevent running injuries through appropriate training. For example, flexibility training and resistance training both help to prevent injuries by increasing muscle elasticity and strength. Incorporating into your weekly schedule rest or cross-training regimens such as cycling, swimming, or rowing instead of running may also help you prevent overuse injuries. Cross-training will also help you maintain cardiovascular fitness if you do become injured and help to speed up the recovery process.

Age itself is a risk factor for the development of osteoarthritis, a form of arthritis associated with the eventual wear and tear on the joint cartilage. Whether or not older runners (as compared to age-matched nonrunners) have an increased incidence of osteoarthritis due to the impact of running is still controversial, at least according to some researchers. Research suggests that older runners may be at more risk to develop osteoarthritis if they have a history of arthritis before they started running, have poor running biomechanics, had a previous injury to a running joint, or are overweight. Some studies suggest that female runners may be more prone to osteoporosis at the knee joint, possibly because of poor biomechanics attributed to the natural angulation of the bones at the knee.

However, when these risk factors are controlled by appropriate research design, most studies indicate that a lifetime of long-distance running is not associated with premature osteoporosis of the ankle, knee, hip, or lower back joints. Actually, several recent studies have reported that runners (ranging in age from 52 to 74) developed musculoskeletal disability at a lower rate over an eight-year period when compared to age-matched nonrunners. This suggests that running may exert a positive effect on musculoskeletal health.

Nevertheless, it might be a good idea to decrease any undesirable stress on the joints as we age. You can help reduce excess loading on the joints by improving the stability of your running gait, possibly through an appropriate biomechanical analysis of your running style. Using stable, well-cushioned shoes and running on soft surfaces may also help reduce joint loading, as will losing excess body fat if you are overweight.

—Mel Williams, PhD,
is the director of the Human Performance Laboratory at Old Dominion University. A runner himself, Mel trains first-time marathoners with the Leukemia Society's "Team In Training" program.
